content.full_text:
May 9, 1978

Mr. Lawrence L. Kelly  
Chairman, Sub-committee  
Citizens Supporting Quality Education  
8240 Hihn Road  
Ben Lomond, California 95005

Dear Mr. Kelly:

Thank you for your letter of May 3, 1978, requesting advice with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. Specifically, you asked whether a member of a board, such as a school board, who was employed by another board member, had a conflict of interests.

As I indicated to you on the telephone, the Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the making or using his official position to influence a governmental decision which may foreseeably and materially affect his financial interest. In the situation described in your letter, the employer would be a source of income, and hence a financial interest, to the employee board member. Thus, the employee would have to disqualify himself from making, participating in the making or using his official position to influence board decisions which could foreseeably and materially affect his employer. In our telephone conversation, you indicated to me that, generally speaking, board decisions would not foreseeably and materially affect the employer. Therefore, the employee board member generally would not be required to disqualify himself from the board's decision-making process.

As I explained to you on the telephone, the Fair Political Practices Commission is empowered to interpret and enforce the provisions of the Political Reform Act. The Commission does not have jurisdiction over the enforcement or interpretation of any other Conflict of Interest laws at the state or local level. Therefore, you may wish to discuss your questions with the county counsel or district counsel to determine if any other conflict of interest laws are applicable.

Lawrence L. Kelly  
May 9, 1978

If I can be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim, Chief  
Conflicts of Interest Division

TP:gb

May 3, 1978

8240 Hihn Road
Ben Lomond, CA 95005

Mr. Michael Bennett
Fair Political Practices Commission
Sacramento, California

Dear Mr. Bennett:

We would like to have an F.P.P.C. opinion on the possible conflict of interest involved in having, as we do, two members of the local School Board with an employer-employee relationship (i.e., financial) in their regular daily work.

This matter is of concern because of the high percentage of tax dollars expended by the Board and possibility of such a relationship influencing School Board decisions.

Thank you for your help in this matter.

Sincerely,

CITIZENS SUPPORTING QUALITY EDUCATION
Chrmn - Sub-committee Lawrence L. Kelly

sections.question: Whether a member of a board, such as a school board, who was employed by another board member, had a conflict of interests.
sections.conclusion: The employee would have to disqualify himself from making, participating in the making or using his official position to influence board decisions which could foreseeably and materially affect his employer. However, in this situation, board decisions would not foreseeably and materially affect the employer, so the employee board member generally would not be required to disqualify himself from the board's decision-making process.
sections.question_synthetic: Does an employee board member who works for another board member have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act that would require disqualification from board decisions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: An employee board member has a conflict of interest regarding decisions that could foreseeably and materially affect their employer, but in this case, general board decisions would not meet that threshold, so disqualification is generally not required. The FPPC notes it does not have jurisdiction over other state or local conflict of interest laws.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (May 3, 1978) and FPPC response (May 9, 1978). No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers, but content clearly presents the question and conclusion. Minor formatting irregularity with date/signature appearing twice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a member of a board, such as a school board, who was employed by another board member, had a conflict of interests.

CONCLUSION: The employee would have to disqualify himself from making, participating in the making or using his official position to influence board decisions which could foreseeably and materially affect his employer. However, in this situation, board decisions would not foreseeably and materially affect the employer, so the employee board member generally would not be required to disqualify himself from the board's decision-making process.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry about conflict of interest involving two school board members with an employer-employee relationship. The response clarifies that disqualification is required only for decisions that foreseeably and materially affect the employer, which generally does not apply to routine board decisions.
